title: The molecular machine behind COVID's drug resistance comes into focus - TCR 05/11/26
description:
Scientists imaged the molecular machine that lets COVID fix its own copying errors - the repair system that's blunted a whole class of antiviral drugs since 2020.
Six African countries can now sequence malaria genomes on a clinic laptop for under $25, with results in five hours.

https://sharedsapience.com/the-century-report-may-11-2026/

This episode covers the COVID self-repair enzyme finally captured in atomic detail and the two drug-design paths it opens; the laptop-scale malaria genome surveillance now running across six African countries; the seawater-tolerant steel that could make ocean-water hydrogen cheap; the Malaysian state of Sarawak building its own AI system on indigenous Sarawak Malay language data with Oxford and Cambridge; and Europe's frontier-AI access standoff, where OpenAI is in active talks to give EU regulators a preview of its cyber model while Anthropic has still not granted Brussels access to Mythos a month after restricted release.

Yang's team captured the enzyme that has limited COVID antivirals since 2020 interacting with remdesivir, sofosbuvir, and bemnifosbuvir at a resolution fine enough to design against. Two pathways open: redesign the analogs so the flawed RNA they generate carries a shape the proofreader cannot recognize, or design a separate drug that locks the proofreader shut. The African consortium's protocol moves continental-scale pathogen surveillance from European and North American centralized facilities onto laptops in the clinics where patients are treated, with a target of 50,000 to 100,000 samples annually. SS-H2 closes the 600 mV gap between where ordinary stainless steel breaks down and where water oxidation actually happens, through a manganese-oxide protective layer the field had treated as a corrosion liability for decades. Sarawak's 2.8 million people now sit behind a sovereign federated AI infrastructure built on indigenous language data. And Thomas Regnier confirmed at a Monday Commission briefing that the architecture of staged release for dangerous capability is being negotiated through a multi-continent verification layer rather than bilateral lab-to-regulator channels.

- Nature Communications - Continental-scale genomic surveillance of Plasmodium falciparum malaria across sub-Saharan Africa: https://www.nature.com/articles/s41467-026-72358-z
- ScienceDaily - "Cannot be explained" - New ultra stainless steel stuns researchers: https://www.sciencedaily.com/releases/2026/05/260510030950.htm
- CNBC - OpenAI in talks with EU over access to new cyber model but Anthropic still holding out on Mythos: https://www.cnbc.com/2026/05/11/openai-eu-cyber-model-anthropic-mythos-gpt.html
